anchor_archetypes.archetype: Exact-match commercial (max 1 per article, deep location page only)
Owner-side action (own pool, used on ~25-30% of articles only)

anchor_archetypes.ratio: as needed when a /boats page is linked

article_structure: ONE HTML ARTICLE IS AUTHORED PER PIECE and reused across all four channels: the generator returns strict JSON {title, html}; telegraph/graph.org consume the HTML directly (img alt attributes are stripped by their API but src+figcaption survive), rentry converts the SAME html to Markdown (h3->##, h4->###, img->![alt](src), figcaption->italic line), LiveJournal posts the raw html. Author for the telegraph tag subset and let the converters degrade gracefully. Allowed tags ONLY: p, h3, h4, ul, ol, li, strong, em, b, i, a, blockquote, figure, img, figcaption, br, hr, aside. NEVER h1/h2/table/div/span/script.

TITLE FIELD (the JSON 'title', NOT an in-body heading): 50-65 characters, British English, no exclamation marks, no em-dashes, no 'ultimate/best-ever' clickbait. Front-load the location noun in the first 5 words and include exactly ONE head term, e.g. 'Boat Rental in Split: Costs, Marinas and Seasons'. Append ONE rotating differentiator from {Costs, Prices, Seasons, Guide, What to Know, 2026, Tips} so 832 locations never share a verbatim title template. The rendered title must be unique corpus-wide (dedupe on title AND on angle|primary-url).

OPENING DIRECT-ANSWER PARAGRAPH (first

, NO heading above it, 40-58 words, NO link inside it): sentence 1 defines/answers the core query and names the location + a concrete departure point + a price band in the correct LOCAL currency, written to be true if quoted in isolation, e.g. 'Renting a boat in Split usually costs EUR 250 to 900 for a day, most trips leave from ACI Marina Split, and you can book online through BoatHire24, a worldwide marketplace to rent boats or list your own.' Embed the primary keyword once in the first 100 characters. End on one orienting fact (season window or typical trip length).

FIRST IMAGE immediately after the opening paragraph: . Prefer a real boathire24 supabase boat photo from config/boathire24_images.json when the article names that boat class; otherwise a relevant Pexels sea/marina shot. Never adjacent to a link.

ORIENTATION SECTION (

'Where to set off in {Location}', ~100-140 words, 2 short

): name 4-6 REAL local entities (actual marinas/ports, bays, islands, beaches, neighbouring towns, landmarks) for that exact place. Place the FIRST contextual link here, deep in a sentence (never the section's first or last sentence), pointing to the PRIMARY location landing page with a partial-match or geo anchor.

BOAT-CHOICE SECTION (

'Which boat to rent in {Location}', ~110-150 words): a lead

then an

    of 4-5 items, each
  1. starting boat type then best-use + rough guest capacity + a 'from' price, e.g. 'Catamaran: seats 10-12 guests, the most stable option for families, from EUR 900 per day.' Tie at least one boat type to the location's real conditions. Place the SECOND link here to a RELEVANT /boats/{slug} page with a descriptive product anchor.

QUESTION-HEADING COST SECTION (<h3> phrased as an exact question, e.g. 'How much does it cost to rent a boat in {Location}?'): first sentence answers in <=25 words with the number, wrapped in <strong> so heading+answer is an extractable Q&A unit, then a <ul> price-band breakdown (shared tour / small motorboat half-day / catamaran full-day / crewed yacht), each <li> one standalone fact with currency. Optionally place a secondary link here to a relevant /blog page with a topic-phrase anchor. Otherwise keep this section clean.

SEASON/LICENCE QUESTION SECTION (<h3>, rotate between 'When is the best time to sail in {Location}?' and 'Do you need a licence to rent a boat in {Location}?' so the footprint varies by article): BLUF answer in sentence 1, then detail. For season: name high-season months, the shoulder value window, and one real local wind (Levante/Poniente, Meltemi, Shamal, Maestral). For licence: state the country PRINCIPLE accurately and defer the exact threshold to 'the operator confirms' rather than inventing a number. Include exactly ONE <blockquote> with a genuine, self-contained local tip.

AT-A-GLANCE block (<h3> 'At a glance' then a 4-6 item <ul>, each 'Label: value'): compress the hard numbers (typical price band, peak months, average trip length, capacity range, currency, licence rule). One dense, highly extractable chunk for snippets and AI retrieval, with parallel grammar in every <li>.

MINI-FAQ near the end (

'Questions about boat rental in {Location}' then 3-4 pairs of

natural-language question

+

answer-first reply of 25-45 words): use REAL long-tail queries (licence, group size, what's included, cancellation/weather, with kids). Each answer a complete self-contained sentence restating the subject, never a bare 'Yes'. Highest-value LLM-citation and People-Also-Ask block. NO links in this section.

CLOSING CTA PARAGRAPH (final

, 35-55 words): summarise the decision, place the LAST link to the home or /become-a-host owner-side target with a branded/naked or owner-action anchor, and include the two-sided sentence (one clause for renters, one inviting owners to list their boat). Owner-side link appears on ONLY ~25-30% of articles; on the rest, end with a renter-only home/branded link. No exclamation, no marketing sign-off.

AUTHOR/EXPERIENCE FOOTER (final element, an <aside> on telegraph/livejournal, italic line on rentry): one consistent sentence, e.g. 'Written by the BoatHire24 team, who book and list verified boats in {Location} and hundreds of destinations worldwide.' Keeps the publisher entity stable without a byline-farm footprint.

HARD LIMITS the validator enforces: 700-950 words with natural variance (do NOT pin every article to one count); 4-6 <h3> sections (reject under 4, over 7); at least one <ul> AND one <ol>; exactly one <blockquote>; 1-2 <figure> images (2 only for 850+ word pieces); British English -ise spellings; zero exclamation marks; zero em-dashes; the primary 'keyword + location' phrase appears in title, opening paragraph, exactly one <h3>, and at most once more in body (cap 4 placements).
llm_optimization: Lead with a self-contained 40-58 word definition+answer paragraph true out of context (names location + BoatHire24 + a hard number), so AI Overviews/Perplexity/ChatGPT can lift one clean passage that fully answers 'how to rent a boat in {Location}' without surrounding context.

Make at least 2 of the <h3>/<h4> headings natural-language questions matching real queries ('How much does it cost to rent a boat in {Location}?', 'Do you need a licence to rent a boat in {Location}?') and answer in the FIRST sentence below, BLUF, in <=25 words wrapped in <strong>, before any elaboration. The question-then-answer pattern is exactly what answer engines extract.

Pack 4-6 EXTRACTABLE, attributable FACTS as atomic one-fact-per-sentence claims, each carrying the location entity: explicit price ranges with the correct local currency (EUR/USD/GBP/AED), season month-windows ('April to October'), capacity numbers ('up to 12 guests'), trip durations ('4 to 8 hours'), distances in nautical miles to a named spot, and the licence principle. Never bury a number mid-clause; never hedge with 'prices vary' without an immediate concrete range.

Maximise ENTITY DENSITY: 6-10 distinct location-tied proper nouns per article (real marinas/ports, bays, islands, beaches, neighbouring towns, the named sea/gulf, local winds) plus 1-2 real boat models that EXIST in BoatHire24's inventory (Azimut, Sunseeker, Lagoon, Fairline, Astondoa, Maiora, Axopar) so the page co-occurs with entities Google's knowledge graph already links to that place.

Define the brand consistently once per article in a citable triple: 'BoatHire24 is a worldwide marketplace where you can rent a boat in {Location} or list your own.' Always one word 'BoatHire24', always 'boathire24.com', always the same spelling of the city, so answer engines consolidate (not split) the entity and name the brand in 'where can I rent a boat in X' answers.

Define one jargon term per article in 'X is Y' form where relevant ('Bareboat charter is renting a boat without a hired skipper, so you operate it yourself'; 'APA is the advance provisioning allowance for fuel and supplies on a crewed yacht'). Crisp definitions are high-value citation chunks.

Use the lists as machine-readable structured blocks with strict parallel grammar (every
  • starts the same way: a noun then a fact): a boat-type
      , a price-band
        , an 'At a glance' label:value
          , and the FAQ. This is what LLMs and AI Overviews parse into bullet answers.

Add a lightweight freshness signal once, naturally ('for the 2026 season' / 'as of 2026'), and rotate the seasonal framing by month so re-crawls see time-relevant content; do not date-stamp every article identically.
Mention 'BoatHire24' as PLAIN TEXT (not only as a link) once or twice, independent of any anchor, so brand co-citation exists separately from the backlink.
Keep paragraphs to 2-4 sentences and one idea each (<=80 words) so embedding/chunking splits land on coherent, self-contained passages and no fact is cut in half.
internal_link_rules: Embed 2-4 links to boathire24.com per article with a WEIGHTED, non-uniform distribution across the corpus (~25% of articles use 2 links, ~50% use 3, ~25% use 4) so a fixed link count is never a footprint. Hard ceiling 4; never 5+. Track last-used anchor per target URL so no anchor repeats on consecutive posts to the same URL, and vary the sentence template around each link.

Mandatory target mix per article: (1) exactly ONE PRIMARY location deep page (the topic city) carrying the strongest descriptive/geo anchor, placed in the orientation or cost section (not the intro, not only the closing line); (2) at most ONE home/core link (home OR /become-a-host) using only branded/naked anchors; (3) at least ONE secondary deep page chosen BY RELEVANCE from a /boats/{slug}, a /blog/{slug}, or a sibling location; (4) optional 4th = a second nearby location only when contextually justified (geo mesh).

Every link resolves to a DISTINCT boathire24.com URL; never point two links in one article at the same URL. If rotation picks a duplicate, skip to the next candidate. Match the secondary deep target to the paragraph topic (a catamaran article links a catamaran/flybridge /boats page; a cost article links a pricing/anchorages /blog page); do NOT link an Azimut boat page from an article about Greek licence rules.

The homepage/apex gets ONLY branded or naked-URL anchors, never exact-match commercial anchors. Exact-match commercial anchors are rationed to AT MOST one per article and point ONLY at the deep location page so the strongest anchor reinforces the most relevant target.

NO link stacking: never two links in the same sentence, the same <li>, or adjacent sentences; keep at least one full paragraph (~120+ words) between any two links. The FAQ and At-a-glance sections carry ZERO links to stay clean and quotable. Place the first link no earlier than mid-first-body-section (out of the title, opening paragraph and the section's first sentence), and vary WHICH section the deep link sits in across articles so the corpus shows no 'link always in paragraph 3' pattern.

Validate at generation time: every chosen URL must exist in config/boathire24_landing_urls.json (locations/blog/boats/core/home), and 'boathire24.com' must appear 2-4 times in the final HTML across distinct URLs; reject and regenerate if outside that range or if any two links share a URL.

Spread link equity: rotate the PRIMARY location across the full 832-location list (keep the deterministic per-channel offset + n_done rotation from _pick_targets so adjacent posts never share a primary or a secondary set), and cap any single target URL to roughly one inbound link per ~20 articles within a rolling window so velocity to one page stays organic.

Map anchor TYPE to target TYPE (see anchor_archetypes): city landing pages get geo/partial anchors; /boats pages get descriptive product anchors; the homepage gets branded/naked anchors; blog targets get topic-phrase anchors. Mismatched (a money anchor pointed at the homepage on every post) is the single biggest footprint to avoid.

Optionally add 1 OUTBOUND plain-text mention of a neutral authority (a port authority, tourism board, or weather/tide service) as TEXT or a single non-competing link in a different section from the money links, to add co-citation trust and break the link-scheme pattern. Never interlink the four satellite channels to each other; anchor text must read as a natural mid-sentence noun phrase, never 'click here' or a dangling line.
image_rules: Embed 1-2 images per article: 1 on 700-820 word posts (after the opening paragraph), a 2nd on 850+ word posts (before the cost or FAQ section, different subject from the first). Vary the count so image quantity is not a constant fingerprint. Never place an image adjacent to a link.

Single markup that degrades correctly on every channel: . Telegraph/graph.org keep src+figcaption (drop alt); rentry renders ![alt](src) then the figcaption as an italic line; LiveJournal keeps the raw figure. src must ALWAYS be an external absolute URL, never relative.

Source preference: (1) if the article names a boat class/model present in config/boathire24_images.json under 'boats' (25 boats with real supabase photos, e.g. azimut-58-white, fairline-targa-43, maiora-26m, limpopo-axopar-28ft), use that boat's real CDN photo and link that same /boats page (image+entity+link alignment); (2) otherwise use a relevant Pexels sea/marina shot. EXPAND the generic Pexels pool (config currently holds only ONE generic URL) so the same stock photo does not appear on hundreds of posts.

ALT TEXT formula (applies to rentry/livejournal and as a filename/relevance signal everywhere): '{boat type or scene} in {Location}, {specific marina/landmark}', under 125 chars, e.g. 'Catamaran anchored off the Pakleni Islands near Split'. Include the location once, the primary keyword at most once, never a keyword-stuffed comma list, never 'image of'.

CAPTION formula (figcaption / italic line): a short human sentence of 6-14 words that ADDS a fact not in the alt and names a spot, boat length or price hook, e.g. 'A 12m day cruiser leaving ACI Marina Split.' British English, no exclamation marks, no em-dashes. Captions are indexed and read by AI, so make them informative.

Match the image to the article subject and location setting (catamaran article -> catamaran; Dubai -> Gulf/skyline marina; fishing -> sportfisher; never a snow/lake shot on a Mediterranean piece). Mismatched stock erodes relevance and trust.

Vary alt and caption WORDING across the corpus via slot-filled templates (rotate boat/city/water-body/marina fillers) so image metadata is not byte-identical network-wide; never reuse the identical (image URL + alt + caption) triple across published articles; track used image URLs per channel the way topics are tracked.

Prefer descriptive hyphenated final path segments where controllable (e.g. catamaran-charter-split-harbour.jpg) for image-search signal, but do not force the exact target keyword into every filename. Validate the external src returns HTTP 200 at publish time; skip/replace rather than ship a broken .
eeat_and_safety: EXPERIENCE: write from a hands-on operator stance with concrete, sensory, lived-in detail only someone who books boats would include (meeting points, morning-vs-afternoon sea state, the offshore breeze building through the day, deposit/refund mechanics, fuel-on-return rules, a real busy weekend, a quieter alternative bay). Generic interchangeable AI boilerplate is the single biggest E-E-A-T and spam risk on these schema-less platforms; specificity is the antidote.

EXPERTISE + ACCURACY: name real entities and define jargon correctly (APA, bareboat, skippered, ICC). Per-country licence/legal claims must be principle-accurate; describe the rule and defer the exact threshold to 'the operator confirms' rather than inventing a false number. Use honest ranges and 'from' figures: NEVER fabricate prices, review counts, star ratings, testimonials with named people, awards, or 'as seen in' claims.

AUTHORITATIVENESS: keep a consistent, verifiable publisher entity (author_name 'BoatHire24' / 'BoatHire24 team', author_url https://boathire24.com) on every post plus the in-body experience footer, so the corpus accrues a recognisable source identity. A small byline rotation ('BoatHire24 Editorial' / 'The BoatHire24 Crew') is acceptable to soften the authorship fingerprint, but keep author_url stable.

FOOTPRINT DIVERSITY: rotate the article SKELETON itself, not just the topic. Maintain 3-4 interchangeable section orderings, 2-3 alternate <h3> phrasings per section, and 5 rotating opening archetypes (direct-answer, scene-set, question, stat-led, scenario), plus vary <ol>-vs-<ul>, whether the optional second image/FAQ appears, and word count within 700-950 so no two posts share a detectable structure. Identical structure at scale is the classic PBN/AI-spam tell.

PER-CHANNEL ANGLE + ANCHOR OFFSET: the four channels must read differently for the same city. Keep distinct angle pools (telegraph = how-to/first-timer, graph.org = experiential day-on-water, rentry = budget/tips, livejournal = practical planning). Track (channel, city, angle) triples and forbid collisions; never publish the same text to two channels; offset the anchor pool per channel so the same target never gets the same anchor across channels on the same day.

UNIQUENESS / DEDUPE hard: dedupe on angle|primary-url AND on rendered title; additionally run an n-gram/near-duplicate check and regenerate any draft that drifts into stock phrasing ('clear water and quiet coves are waiting') or overlaps heavily with a recent post on the same city.

VELOCITY + TIMING: do not burst all 26 daily posts (10 graph.org + 10 rentry + 3 telegraph + 3 livejournal) at one timestamp or all at one landing page. Drip across the day with randomised gaps, spread the day's posts across different primary locations and secondary targets, and RAMP new target pages slowly (a brand-new /city page gets 1-2 backlinks/week, not 10 on day one) to mirror organic accrual.

HELPFULNESS over keyword density: each article must genuinely answer the renter's real questions (cost, licence, season, departure point, what's included). The ~4-placement primary-keyword cap and the 2-4 link cap keep the text reading for humans, which is what survives Helpful-Content and spam updates.

PRE-PUBLISH QUALITY GATE (hard reject + regenerate, not a warning): reject any draft that is <700 words, has <4 <h3>, has <4 distinct location proper nouns, links the homepage more than once, has any two links sharing a URL, exceeds 4 total boathire24 links, uses any exact-match anchor on the apex, or contains an exclamation mark or em-dash, or fails the British-English -ise lint.
deepseek_prompt_template:
"You are an expert British boating and travel writer producing one original, genuinely useful article for BoatHire24, a worldwide marketplace to rent boats and to list your own. Output STRICT JSON only: {"title": "...", "html": "..."}. No prose outside the JSON. ARTICLE BRIEF Location: {location} Angle: {angle} Local currency to quote: {currency} Body of water / sea: {sea} Real local entities you MUST weave in (use 6-10 naturally, do not list them mechanically): {entities} Year: {year} LINKS - embed EXACTLY {link_count} links to boathire24.com, each in a DIFFERENT sentence and a DIFFERENT section, each a DISTINCT URL, each with DIFFERENT natural anchor text. Use these exact URLs and the assigned anchor for each: 1. PRIMARY (place in the orientation or cost section, deep inside a sentence, not its first or last sentence): {primary_url} | anchor: {primary_anchor} (e.g. 'rent a boat in {location}' or 'compare verified boat listings') 2. SECONDARY deep page (place in the boat-choice or cost section, must match the topic): {secondary_url} | anchor: {secondary_anchor} (descriptive product or topic phrase, e.g. 'the {boat_name} listing' or 'a guide to local anchorages') 3. HOME/OWNER (place ONLY in the closing paragraph; include only if link_count >= 3): {home_or_host_url} | anchor: branded or naked (e.g. 'BoatHire24' or 'boathire24.com'); if this is the owner CTA use 'list your boat' or 'become a host'. {extra_link_line} NEVER stack two links in one sentence, one list item, or adjacent sentences. Keep at least a full paragraph between links. The apex homepage may use ONLY a branded or naked anchor, never an exact-match money phrase. Use at most ONE exact-match commercial anchor in the whole article and only on the PRIMARY location link. IMAGE - embed {image_count} image(s) using EXACTLY this markup, with an EXTERNAL src, placed right after the opening paragraph (and, for a second image, before the cost section), never next to a link: If a second image is requested use this second src: {image_url_2} with its own alt {image_alt_2}. STRUCTURE (use the telegraph tag subset ONLY: p, h3, h4, ul, ol, li, strong, em, b, i, a, blockquote, figure, img, figcaption, br, hr, aside - NO h1, h2, table, div, span, script): - TITLE field: 50-65 chars, location in the first 5 words, one head term, one differentiator from {Costs, Prices, Seasons, Guide, What to Know, 2026, Tips}. No clickbait, no exclamation, no em-dash. Specific to {location}. - Open with a single

of 40-58 words that DIRECTLY answers the angle: name {location}, a real departure point, and a price band in {currency}, and name BoatHire24 once. It must read true if quoted alone. NO link and NO heading in this first paragraph. Put the primary keyword in the first 100 characters. - Then the first
per_channel_render_notes: SHARED MODEL: author ONE {title, html} per article for the telegraph tag subset; all four channels consume the same html. The existing publishers handle per-channel conversion, so the generator must NOT emit channel-specific markup beyond the allowed tag set. Differentiate channels via the ANGLE pool and anchor offset, not via different HTML for the same city.

graph.org / telegra.ph (telegraph_publish.py html_to_nodes): renders the curated tag subset via the Telegraph API. h1/h2->h3, h5/h6->h4, div->p, span unwrapped; only href/src attributes are kept, so is DROPPED (still write alt for the other channels and as a filename signal) but

rentry.co (rentry_post.py _ToMarkdown): converts the same html to Markdown - h3->'##', h4->'###', strong/b->**, em/i->*, blockquote->'> ',
    /
      to bullet/numbered lists, ->![alt](src),

LiveJournal (livejournal_post.py): posts the raw html article directly, so figure/img/figcaption, lists, blockquote and aside render natively. No conversion needed; the same allowed-tag html is valid here.

All channels: keep src external and absolute (supabase CDN or Pexels) and validate HTTP 200 before publish. Keep the <aside> author footer (renders as an aside on telegraph/livejournal; the rentry converter falls through it as plain text, which is acceptable). Maintain the existing drip caps (10 graph.org + 10 rentry + 3 telegraph + 3 livejournal per day) and stagger publish times rather than bursting.

TARGET SOURCES are the keys in config/boathire24_landing_urls.json: locations (832), blog (361), boats (41), core (6: home, become-a-host, blog, faq, how-it-works, search), home (1). Boat images come from config/boathire24_images.json -> boats (25 real photos) and generic (currently ONE Pexels URL, expand this pool). The generator must pull only URLs/images that actually exist in these files.
quality_checklist: Word count is 700-950 (reject otherwise) and varies from the previous post on the same channel (not pinned to one number).
Exactly 4-6 <h3> sections; at least 2 are natural-language question headings, and the first sentence under each question answers it in <=25 words wrapped in <strong>.

Opening: first block is a

of 40-58 words, contains the location name + a price band in the correct local currency + 'BoatHire24', contains NO link and NO heading, and reads true in isolation.

Contains at least one
    AND one
      , exactly one
      , one 'At a glance' label:value list, and a 3-4 pair

      +

      FAQ block.

Link count is 2-4; 'boathire24.com' appears 2-4 times; every link is a DISTINCT URL that exists in boathire24_landing_urls.json; exactly one PRIMARY location deep link; the homepage/apex is linked at most once and only with a branded/naked anchor; at most one exact-match commercial anchor and only on the primary location link.

No two links in the same sentence, same <li>, or adjacent sentences; at least one full paragraph between links; the FAQ and At-a-glance sections contain zero links; the first link is not in the title, opening paragraph, or a section's first sentence.

Every anchor text is unique within the article and drawn from a different archetype (branded / naked / partial / geo / product), and does not repeat the last anchor used for that same target URL on that channel.

1-2 <figure> images with external src + non-empty alt + a 6-14 word caption that differs from the alt; image subject matches the location/boat-type; image src returns HTTP 200 and is not a duplicate of a recently used image on that channel.

At least 4 distinct location proper nouns (target 6-10) and 1-2 real boat models that exist in the inventory; at least 4 extractable facts (price range with currency, season month-window, capacity, duration/distance).

British English -ise spellings; ZERO exclamation marks; ZERO em-dashes; no h1/h2/table/div/span/script tags; no 'click here'/'read more' anchors; no fabricated prices, reviews, ratings, awards, or named testimonials.

Title is 50-65 chars, location in first 5 words, no clickbait/exclamation/em-dash, and is unique corpus-wide; the (angle|primary-url) key is not already in backlink_topics_used.json for this channel; the body is not a near-duplicate of a recent same-city post on another channel.
